title: Held-out boundary scenario under the same artifact discipline
description: A held-out scenario outside the original A-D set that still runs under the same artifact and acceptance discipline.
query: What integration boundary keeps geometry independent in v1 while still letting CIMA consume structural signals?

need_proposal.reason: Need the held-out boundary rule plus the read-side consumption path.

conclusions.content: Geometry stays independent in v1 and CIMA consumes its structural signals via Kafka and Qdrant or read models rather than runtime RPC.

llm.answer: The held-out boundary keeps geometry as a separate service while CIMA consumes structural signals through Kafka updates and Qdrant or read-model state, not runtime RPC. [S1] [S2] [P1]

documents.title: Geometry boundary decision
documents.text: Geometry is a separate service in v1. It does not own turn control or context assembly. Its responsibility is limited to structural signals such as core items, bridge candidates and cluster state.

documents.title: Consumption path note
documents.text: CIMA consumes geometry through Kafka-fed read models and Qdrant payload flags. Runtime assembly must not depend on synchronous RPC into the geometry service.

documents.title: Noise document
documents.text: This note discusses container images, package caches and unrelated deployment trivia that should not affect the held-out boundary answer. filler filler filler filler filler filler filler filler.

citems.content: Geometry remains a separate service and does not own turn control or context assembly.
CIMA consumes geometry through Kafka-fed read models and Qdrant payload flags instead of runtime RPC.

summaries.content: The v1 boundary keeps geometry independent while exposing structural signals to CIMA through asynchronous and read-side channels.
